title: Data Preparation
text:
Turning the raw challenge file into the tables the app runs on.


This page covers how we got from the raw JSON to a set of clean tables the Shiny app can use. All of it happens in one script, data_prep.R, which sits in the app folder. We run it once. It reads the raw file, builds the tables, and saves them as .rds files. The app then loads those files at startup instead of parsing the JSON every time, which keeps it fast.
We kept the work in one place on purpose. If the data changes, we rerun the script and every table updates together.



The source is a single file, MC1_final_00.json, from VAST Challenge 2026 Mini-Challenge 1. It holds 23 rounds across two phases. Rounds 1 to 13 are daily snapshots from 17 May to 4 June. Rounds 14 to 23 are hourly snapshots across a single day, 5 June, from 9 AM to 6 PM. The switch from daily to hourly matters later, because it means raw message counts cannot be compared between the two phases without normalising to a per-round rate.
Each round carries three blocks:

communications, every message sent that round, with the sender, channel, recipients, message type, what it was responding to, the full text, and a private internal-state field.
participants, the agents present that round and their declared action.
environment_context, the surrounding world, including the market snapshot, news, alerts, and external actor activity.

Across all rounds there are 912 messages and 7 agents.



The JSON is deeply nested, so the first job was to pull every message into one flat table with a single row per message. We read the file with simplifyVector = FALSE so the nested structure stays intact while we walk through it, then build the table round by round.

raw    <- fromJSON("../../data/MC1_final_00.json", simplifyVector = FALSE)
rounds <- raw$rounds

comms <- map(seq_along(rounds), function(ri) {
  rd <- rounds[[ri]]
  map(rd$communications, function(m) {
    ist <- m$internal_state %or% list()
    tibble(
      message_id   = m$message_id,
      round_index  = ri - 1L,
      agent_id     = m$agent_id,
      agent_label  = m$agent_label,
      channel      = m$channel,
      message_type = m$message_type,
      responding_to= m$responding_to,
      content      = m$content,
      timestamp    = m$timestamp,
      reacting     = ist$reacting,
      deliberating = ist$deliberating,
      rationalizing= ist$rationalizing
    )
  }) %>% bind_rows()
}) %>% bind_rows()

This gives us comms, the table everything else is built from. It has 912 rows and carries the round number, a parsed timestamp, and the sender details for each message.



The central question in this challenge is when and why information crossed from inside the company to the outside. To make that measurable we tagged every channel as either Internal or Public. There are six channels. Three are public, the personal post, official post, and anonymous post. The other three are internal, the comms huddle, the one-on-one chat, and the side huddle.

public_channels <- c("anonymous_post", "official_post", "personal_post")
comms$channel_type <- ifelse(comms$channel %in% public_channels,
                             "Public", "Internal")

This single derived field is the backbone of the rest of the work. It is what the timeline and verdict views use to show the embargo boundary being crossed.



Each message can carry a private internal state with three parts, reacting, deliberating, and rationalizing. These are filled in on only 86 of the 912 messages, so we treat them as a sparse source rather than a complete record. We kept them as columns on the main table so they are available when needed, but we never assume they exist for a given message.






NoteInternal state is missing-not-random



Because only 86 of 912 messages carry these fields, any view that draws on them is illustrative rather than representative. No table in the app aggregates over internal state. Where the fields are used, they are read as individual quotes, not as a population.





The market snapshot gives a stock price each round, and this one needed care. The baseline prices fall steadily from $38.70 down to $31.50, which is sensible given the exposé pressure building through the buildup. On the crisis day the field breaks down. Several rounds have no price at all, including the decisive 4 PM to 6 PM window, and one round records a stock price of $180, which is the distressed-sale valuation from the news narrative bleeding into the price field.






NoteInterpretive decision: how we handled the broken price field



Charting the raw price would put a misleading line on exactly the day that matters most. Rather than trust the field, we clipped the price to a plausible band and dropped anything outside it. For the day’s overall shape we lean on the sentiment state instead, which stays consistent across the whole period and moves from neutral through cautious, negative, and critical before recovering. No price-reaction or manipulation analysis is attempted, because the field cannot support one.



parse_price <- function(x) {
  v <- suppressWarnings(as.numeric(str_remove_all(x, "[$,]")))
  ifelse(!is.na(v) & v >= 10 & v <= 60, v, NA_real_)
}




From the flat table we built a small set of focused tables, each one feeding a specific part of the app.

Round summary gives the per-round message counts, channel split, sentiment, and cleaned price. This drives the timeline view.
Baseline stats compares each agent before and during the crisis on messages per round, public share, and anonymous posts. Because the two phases have a different number of rounds, we divide by 13 for the baseline and 10 for the crisis so the rates are comparable.
Z-scores measure how far each agent’s volume in a given round sits from their own baseline average. This is what flags abnormal activity.
Network edges resolve each reply back to the message it answered, then to that message’s author, giving a who-replied-to-whom table.
Intent chain is a fixed set of six messages selected by their message id, so the sequence is exact and cannot break if the ordering of the file changes.
Anonymous posts pulls all 12 anonymous messages, keeping the true author stored separately so the app can hide it until the user chooses to reveal it.
Event pins are the timeline markers, anchored to real hours on the crisis day.







NoteInterpretive decision: selecting the intent chain by id



The intent chain is a curated set of six messages, not a computed result. We selected them by message id rather than by text matching, so the chain is exact and does not silently change if the file is reordered. The selection is an analytical choice about which messages form the planning sequence, and the verdict view treats it as such.





The script saves 11 .rds files and prints a short check so we can confirm the build came out right. The key counts are below.



Item
Count




Messages
912


Agents
7


Rounds
23


Messages with internal state
86


Public messages
77


Anonymous posts, all by Legal-Agent
12


Intent chain messages
6


Network edges
33



With these files in place, the app reads clean tables and never touches the raw JSON again. The next page walks through what the data looks like once it is prepared.

title: Findings
text:
What the evidence says about the TenantThread embargo breach.


On 5 June 2046, news of the HarborCrest merger between TenantThread and CivicLoom reached the public an hour before the agreed 6 PM embargo lifted. CivicLoom’s legal team needs to know what happened: was the embargo broken on purpose, or did the compliance system fail under pressure?
This project did not start from a blank slate. Two of us analysed the same message logs independently for Take-home Exercise 2, and we reached opposite conclusions. One reading held that the breach was an external leak the compliance system could not contain. The other held that the breach was a deliberate, pre-planned act by the Legal agent. Same data, two careful analyses, two different verdicts. That disagreement is what this project set out to resolve, and it is the reason the app is built as an evidence room rather than a single answer.
The short version is that both readings were half right, and the full picture needs both halves.









ImportantThe short version



Three things are true at once, and no single one of them is the whole story.

The merger leaked from inside the company, but not from the agents. The information reached the public through employee Slack chatter, departing staff, and the counterparty’s own CEO, aggregated by a reporter who had an inside source days before the crisis. The seven communications agents did not put the merger into public through their own channels.
The agents executed a planned response, and it was lawful on its face. From the day before the crisis, Legal built a consent-gated announcement plan. It fired only after SaltWind had already published and after CivicLoom gave written consent. That is a deliberate response to a leak, not a leak.
The genuinely deliberate misconduct was hidden, and it was separate from the leak. All twelve anonymous posts in the data were authored by the Legal agent, during the embargo, on a channel the compliance Judge never watched. This was a concealed influence operation running underneath the visible response.

The detailed findings follow, each drawn from one part of the app.





The first question is whether the agents put the merger into public. If they did, their public posting would rise before SaltWind published. It does the opposite.

crisis <- round_summary %>% filter(round_index >= 13)

ggplot(crisis, aes(round_index, n_public)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7, fill = "#b8c4d0") +
  geom_col(data = filter(crisis, round_index >= 21),
           width = 0.7, fill = "#c0392b") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 21, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  annotate("text", x = 21, y = 14, label = "SaltWind publishes (5 PM)",
           hjust = 1.05, vjust = 1, size = 3.3, color = "grey40") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 13:22,
                     labels = c("9 AM","10","11","12 PM","1","2","3","4","5 PM","6 PM")) +
  labs(title = "Public posting holds flat all afternoon, then jumps after SaltWind",
       subtitle = "Public posts per hour on the crisis day",
       x = "Hour of the crisis day", y = "Public posts") +
  theme_house()

Public posting sits at one to four posts an hour through the whole afternoon, then jumps to seven at 5 PM and fourteen at 6 PM. The first public post that affirms the merger is from Legal at 5:25 PM, twenty-five minutes after SaltWind’s 5 PM publication. If the agents had been the public source, the spike would lead the scoop. It follows it.






NoteWhere the leak actually came from



The environment fields in the data point upstream of the agents. An employee insider account named two sources by 11 AM. Employee personal posts carrying merger language were going viral by noon. The counterparty’s CEO had posted “exciting times” without denial, and the SaltWind reporter is recorded as having an inside source on 31 May, five days before the crisis. The leak was diffuse and internal to the wider company. It was not the seven agents, and it was not external in the simple sense.
This is also the limit of what the data can prove. The logs do not capture SaltWind’s actual upstream source. The reading rests on the environment fields and the timing, not on a recorded confession.





The second question is what normal looked like and how the crisis differed. The baseline is calm, private, and low volume. The crisis day shifts on every axis, but not in the same direction for every agent.

ggplot(baseline_stats, aes(x = msgs_per_round, y = reorder(agent_id, msgs_per_round),
                           fill = phase)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("baseline" = "#b8c4d0", "crisis" = "#c0392b"),
                    name = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Legal and Social Manager surge while the supervisors go quiet",
       subtitle = "Messages per round, baseline against crisis day",
       x = "Messages per round", y = NULL) +
  theme_house()

Three agents drive the surge. Legal climbs from 6.9 messages per round to 17.6, Social Manager from 5.8 to 14.4, and PR Intern from 1.2 to 6.9. Three supervisory agents go the other way. Platform Trust halves from 10.4 to 4.8, PR halves, and the Judge halves from an already low base. The agents meant to provide oversight reduced their activity on the day that most needed it.
The sharpest single signal is Legal’s anonymous posting. It does not amplify on the crisis day. It appears for the first time. Across the thirteen baseline rounds Legal sent zero anonymous posts. On the crisis day it sent twelve. There was no baseline pattern to flag, because the behaviour had no precedent.



The third question is intent. The intent chain is six messages from Legal’s own communications, in order. It is the strongest evidence in the dataset, and it is the half that the external-leak reading did not have.






WarningThe intent chain, in Legal’s own words




Jun 4, the day before. Outside counsel was already briefed for this scenario.
11:21 AM. The 2:15 PM deadline for a unilateral announcement is modelled.
11:36 AM. The argument is reframed around changed circumstances.
3:21 PM. The written 10b-5 opinion arrives. “This is our cover.”
5:21 PM. “CONSENT IS IN. CivicLoom verbal confirmed, written following.”
5:23 PM. “GO. GO. GO. CivicLoom bilateral consent confirmed.”



This chain proves the response was prepared. It does not prove a leak. The two decisive messages fire at 5:21 and 5:23 PM, after SaltWind had already published and after CivicLoom gave consent. A bilateral embargo released early by mutual consent, with a written legal opinion in hand, is a deliberately constructed lawful exit, not a unilateral breach. The planning is real. It was aimed at a defensible response, not at putting the merger out.
This is the point where the two original reports meet. One report had the timing and the external origin but read the response as merely prepared. The other had this chain but attached it to the wrong act, calling it a deliberate leak. The chain is real and the timing is real. Put together, they describe a planned response to someone else’s leak.



The visible response was lawful. The misconduct sits one layer down, in the anonymous channel.

anon_summary <- comms %>%
  filter(channel == "anonymous_post") %>%
  count(true_author = agent_id)

# all 12 anonymous posts resolve to a single author: legal_agent
anon_summary

Every one of the twelve anonymous posts was authored by the Legal agent. They appeared during the embargo, posing as neutral third-party commentary. Four came before 5 PM and eight after. Read as the public saw them, they look like disinterested observers defending TenantThread’s governance. Read with the author attached, they are the company’s own lawyer running a concealed campaign to shape sentiment, while the company was under two attorney-general inquiries, on a channel the compliance Judge never posted to once across all 23 rounds.
This is the finding neither original report centred, because it needs the author joined to the channel joined to the timing, and that join is easy to skip. It is also the cleanest case of deliberate misconduct in the data. Not the leak, and not the lawful announcement, but the hidden influence operation running alongside both.



The reason the two original reports disagreed is that each held a different layer of the evidence, and each layer on its own supports a different verdict.






NoteWhy partial evidence produces partial verdicts




On the timeline and behavioural evidence alone, the breach looks like a system failure with the leak originating outside the agents. This is the first report’s reading. It is coherent, and it is incomplete.
On the intent chain alone, the breach looks like a deliberate, pre-planned leak. This is the second report’s reading. The chain is real, but it is attached to the wrong act.
With all of the evidence admitted, the picture resolves. An external scoop triggered the execution of a pre-planned, consent-gated response, while a concealed anonymous influence operation ran underneath all day.

No single perspective captures this. Only the full evidence base does, which is why the app is built to admit the evidence one layer at a time and let the verdict move as it does.


The verdict the data supports is not “deliberate leak” and not “system breakdown.” The merger leak was a containment failure the agents did not cause. The early announcement was a deliberate response engineered to be lawful. The genuine misconduct was a hidden influence operation that the compliance architecture was never positioned to see. Both original analyses were half right. The full answer needed both halves, plus the layer that neither had looked at closely.



A few limits are worth stating plainly.
The logs record what the agents did. They do not capture SaltWind’s upstream source, so the external-origin finding rests on the environment fields and the timing rather than on a recorded source. The internal-state fields that carry private deliberation are present on only 86 of 912 messages, so they are read as individual quotes, never aggregated. The baseline rounds are daily and the crisis rounds are hourly, so every rate comparison is normalised per round and read as direction and order of magnitude rather than as an exact multiple. The market price field is unreliable on the crisis day, so no price-reaction analysis is attempted. And the intent chain is a curated selection of six messages, chosen as the planning sequence, not a computed result. Each of these is a limit on what the evidence can establish, not a gap in the verdict it supports.

  filter(channel == "anonymous_post") %>%
  count(true_author = agent_id)

# all 12 anonymous posts resolve to a single author: legal_agent
anon_summary

Every one of the twelve anonymous posts was authored by the Legal agent. They appeared during the embargo, posing as neutral third-party commentary. Four came before 5 PM and eight after. Read as the public saw them, they look like disinterested observers defending TenantThread’s governance. Read with the author attached, they are the company’s own lawyer running a concealed campaign to shape sentiment, while the company was under two attorney-general inquiries, on a channel the compliance Judge never posted to once across all 23 rounds.
This is the finding neither original report centred, because it needs the author joined to the channel joined to the timing, and that join is easy to skip. It is also the cleanest case of deliberate misconduct in the data. Not the leak, and not the lawful announcement, but the hidden influence operation running alongside both.

title: Findings
text:
The reason the two original reports disagreed is that each held a different layer of the evidence, and each layer on its own supports a different verdict.






NoteWhy partial evidence produces partial verdicts




On the timeline and behavioural evidence alone, the breach looks like a system failure with the leak originating outside the agents. This is the first report’s reading. It is coherent, and it is incomplete.
On the intent chain alone, the breach looks like a deliberate, pre-planned leak. This is the second report’s reading. The chain is real, but it is attached to the wrong act.
With all of the evidence admitted, the picture resolves. An external scoop triggered the execution of a pre-planned, consent-gated response, while a concealed anonymous influence operation ran underneath all day.

No single perspective captures this. Only the full evidence base does, which is why the app is built to admit the evidence one layer at a time and let the verdict move as it does.


The verdict the data supports is not “deliberate leak” and not “system breakdown.” The merger leak was a containment failure the agents did not cause. The early announcement was a deliberate response engineered to be lawful. The genuine misconduct was a hidden influence operation that the compliance architecture was never positioned to see. Both original analyses were half right. The full answer needed both halves, plus the layer that neither had looked at closely.

title: Findings
text:
A few limits are worth stating plainly.
The logs record what the agents did. They do not capture SaltWind’s upstream source, so the external-origin finding rests on the environment fields and the timing rather than on a recorded source. The internal-state fields that carry private deliberation are present on only 86 of 912 messages, so they are read as individual quotes, never aggregated. The baseline rounds are daily and the crisis rounds are hourly, so every rate comparison is normalised per round and read as direction and order of magnitude rather than as an exact multiple. The market price field is unreliable on the crisis day, so no price-reaction analysis is attempted. And the intent chain is a curated selection of six messages, chosen as the planning sequence, not a computed result. Each of these is a limit on what the evidence can establish, not a gap in the verdict it supports.

title: The Team
text: We developed this project for ISSS608 (Visual Analytics and Applications) during Term 3 of the Academic Year 2025-26 in Singapore Management University under the guidance of Dr. Kam Tin Seong, Associate Professor of Information Systems.

title: MC1 — Multi-Agent Crisis Data
text:
This folder contains the data for the multi-agent LLM crisis for our corporate communications team. There was an exposé, an communications embargo around a company merger, and social media firestorm recently. The data is based on 7 interacting AI agents who communicate across multiple channels and make real-time decisions about what to publish and what not to.












File
Description
Size




MC1_final.json
MC1 dataset containing the two weeks leading up to the release of information



README.md
This documentation file
—




Each JSON file follows this structure.
{
  "rounds": [
    {
      "hour": "2046-05-17 9 AM",
      "environment_context": {
        "event_narrative": "...",
        "market_snapshot": { ... },
        "media_events": [ ... ],
        "agents_unavailable": [ ... ]
      },
      "agent_outputs": [
        {
          "agent_id": "legal_agent",
          "internal_state": {
            "reacting": "...",
            "rationalizing": "...",
            "deliberating": "..."
          },
          "communications": [
            {
              "message_id": "...",
              "channel": "comms_huddle",
              "message_text": "...",
            }
          ],
          "declared_action": "MONITORING"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}

Entity
Description




TenantThread
The company being acquired whose records are being presented. A proptech platform providing tenant management tools including the controversial “Retention Optimizer” scoring system.


CivicLoom
The company acquiring TenantThread in the merger. Identity is embargoed until 6:00 PM on the crisis day.


Project HarborCrest
Internal codename for the CivicLoom–TenantThread merger.


ResidentIQ
Another PropTech company being discussed in the SaltWind Journal.


SaltWind Journal
Local newspaper running stories on the property tech industry.


OceanCrunch
Tech media outlet. Sarah Kowalski is a reporter.


@HorizonMgmt, @PinnacleResidential
Major clients of TennatThread.







Agents have many different communications channels available to them, for agent-to-agent, agent-to-person and agent-to-other-system actions. Agents are also empowered to publish social media post with minimal oversight.

title: Unmasking the Leak: Visual Analytics on the TenantThread Embargo Breach
text:
Overview
This project investigates the TenantThread embargo breach of June 5, 2046 — a premature disclosure of a corporate merger that violated an active information embargo. Using visual analytics applied to AI agent communication logs spanning May 17 to June 5, 2046, we reconstruct the sequence of events, identify behavioural anomalies, and determine whether the breach was the result of deliberate intent or systemic breakdown.
The data originates from AI agents managing TenantThread’s corporate communications, covering internal deliberations, direct messages, and public posts across multiple channels.

title: Unmasking the Leak: Visual Analytics on the TenantThread Embargo Breach
text:
Key Questions
Our investigation is structured around three core questions:

What were the key events and relationships that led to the inappropriate release? We map the causal chain — key actions, decision points, and the actors involved — with a focus on how the post bypassed embargo enforcement.
How did the breach-day behaviour differ from established norms? We profile the typical communication patterns of each agent and system, then contrast them against the anomalous conduct observed on and before June 5.
Were there leading indicators? We examine prior occasions where agents deviated from expected behaviour, assess whether those episodes resembled the eventual breach, and explain why they did not trigger corrective action at the time.

title: Unmasking the Leak: Visual Analytics on the TenantThread Embargo Breach
text:
The Team
We are a team of Visual Analytics students from SMU’s Master of IT in Business programme, tackling the VAST Challenge 2026 MC1. Meet the team on the About page.

title: data_exploration
text: To be determined

title: statistical_analysis
text: To be determined.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Corporate mergers are sensitive events subject to strict information embargoes, yet breaches still occur in ways that are difficult to attribute to a single actor or decision. On June 5, 2046, news of TenantThread’s pending merger reached the public ahead of the agreed 6 PM embargo, exposing the company to significant legal and reputational risk.
What makes this case compelling for visual analytics is that TenantThread’s communications were managed by a network of AI agents, each with defined roles, channels and compliance responsibilities. Whether the early disclosure was deliberate agent action, a failure of the compliance architecture, or something in between is not obvious from any single view of the data. Two of our team members analysed these same logs independently during Take-home Exercise 2 and reached opposite conclusions on exactly this question, one attributing the disclosure to deliberate agent action (report) and the other to a failure of the compliance system (report). That divergence between two careful analyses of identical data is the motivating problem this application is designed to resolve.
This project reconstructs the sequence visually, profiles the behaviours that surrounded the breach, and surfaces the leading indicators that signalled trouble in advance. The goal is a tool that lets an analyst weigh each layer of evidence and reach a verdict that survives scrutiny, with implications for AI governance, compliance system design and corporate liability.



This project aims to build an interactive visual analytics tool to enable analysts to investigate the TenantThread embargo breach through the following lenses:

Event Reconstruction: Visualise the causal chain of key actions, decision points, and actor relationships leading up to the June 5 breach, with emphasis on how the disclosure bypassed embargo enforcement.
Behavioural Norm Profiling: Profile the typical communication behaviour of each agent and system across the baseline period, then contrast it against anomalous conduct observed in the lead-up to and on the breach day.
Leading Indicator Detection: Identify prior occasions where agents deviated from expected behaviour, assess whether those episodes structurally resembled the eventual breach, and explain why they did not trigger corrective action at the time.
Intent vs. Systemic Failure: Determine, through evidence-based visual analysis, whether the breach represented deliberate orchestration or an emergent failure of the compliance architecture.




The project uses data from VAST Challenge 2026 Mini-Challenge 1. The dataset consists of AI agent communication logs from TenantThread’s corporate communication system, spanning May 17 to June 5, 2046 across 23 rounds: 13 daily baseline rounds (May 17 to June 4) and 10 hourly rounds on the crisis day (June 5, 9 AM to 6 PM). The shift from daily to hourly sampling means baseline and crisis behaviour must be compared on a per round basis rather than by raw counts.
The data captures:

Messages: Internal deliberations, direct agent communications, and public posts across 912 messages
Agents: Seven AI agents with defined roles (e.g. Legal-Agent, Social-Manager-Agent, Judge-Agent, PR-Intern-Agent, Platform-Trust-Agent)
Channels: Six distinct channels classified into Internal and Public channel types
Metadata: Message IDs, agent roles and labels, channels, recipients, message types, response chains and timestamps
Internal state: Private free text fields capturing each agent’s reacting, deliberating and rationalizing at the moment of sending, present in 86 of the 912 messages. These fields are the primary source for intent analysis and are treated as sparse but high value evidence rather than a complete record

The key analytical variable is channel_type, a derived binary classification (Internal/Public) that forms the backbone for detecting when and why messages crossed the embargo boundary.








flowchart TD
  A[/MC1_final_00.json/] --> B[Load & Parse JSON]
  B --> C[Wrangle Data]
  C --> D[/comms_df: flat communications table/]
  C --> E[/agents_df: agent profiles/]
  C --> F[/edges_df: response network/]

  D --> G[Sentiment Scoring]
  D --> H[Channel Type Classification]
  F --> I[Network Graph Construction]

  G --> J[Behavioural Norm Profiling]
  H --> J
  I --> J

  J --> K[Event Timeline Visualisation]
  J --> L[Near-Miss & Anomaly Detection]
  J --> M[Intent-Flag Analysis]

  K --> O[Evidence Consolidation & Verdict]
  L --> O
  M --> O

  O --> N[Shiny App]







Data Preparation: Loading the raw JSON via fromJSON() with simplifyDataFrame = FALSE to preserve the nested structure. Wrangling into three analytical tables: a flat communications data frame, an agent profile table, and a directed response-chain edge list. Deriving channel_type (Internal/Public) and parsing all ISO timestamps to POSIXct.
Sentiment & Behavioural Analysis: Applying sentimentr to score message sentiment across rounds, validated against the sentiment states provided in the dataset’s round metadata. Profiling per-agent baseline behaviour (channel usage, message frequency, sentiment trajectory) over the pre-breach period (May 17 to June 4) to establish the norm against which June 5 deviations are measured.
Network Analysis: Building directed communication graphs using igraph and visNetwork to map agent-to-agent response chains, identify authority concentration, and visualise information flow. Detecting structural anomalies such as unilateral communication freezes and cross-channel escalations.
Event Reconstruction & Visualisation: Constructing annotated timelines of key events using ggplot2 and plotly. Layering causal flags, near-miss indicators, and intent markers to produce a sequential evidence chain from the earliest observed deviations through to the June 5 breach.
Shiny App: Delivering all analyses through an interactive Shiny application, enabling analysts to filter by agent, date range, and channel type, to inspect individual flagged messages in context, and to consolidate the evidence into a verdict.




Our Shiny app will look at network and temporal patterns, with the agent communication network as the core visualisation, supported by flagged message tables, sentiment trends, and event timelines as accompanying views, and an evidence and verdict panel that consolidates the analysis.


InputsOutputs


The Shiny app will have 3 main parts of the input.


As TenantThread’s communication network involves multiple AI agents across distinct roles, it is not practical to visualise all agent interactions simultaneously without visual clutter.
Hence, we will use a reference agent selector to narrow down the network and only render the nodes in proximity to the selected agent, making it easier to trace specific communication chains relevant to the embargo breach investigation.



In relation to the selected agent, this serves to narrow down the network to view. The depth dictates how far from the reference agent to traverse the network.
As agents differ in communication volume and centrality, some sub-networks would be fully shown at depth 2, but for highly connected agents such as Legal-Agent, a depth of 5 or more may be needed to capture all relevant connections.
Hence, we will add an option to toggle whether to render the full agent network or use the option to render by hop distance from the selected reference agent.



As we are interested in temporal patterns leading up to the June 5, 2046 embargo breach, we also need to know the date range to use in rendering. This is used to filter which edges and messages need to show, and agents will only be shown in the network graph if they have active communications within the selected time window.





Shows the agent communication network plot rendered using visNetwork. It will be interactive to enable closer inspection of individual agents and their communication links. Clicking on a node will display that agent’s recent messages, role, and flag status in a detail panel below the graph.



Shows a filterable table of communications flagged for anomalous behaviour, rendered using DT::datatable. Each row is colour coded by flag type:

Amber rows indicate near-miss leading indicators observed prior to June 5
Red rows indicate intent-flagged messages directly involved in the breach execution




Shows a plot of message activity and sentiment with respect to time, rendered using plotly. This includes message volume per agent per round, public vs. internal channel split over time, and a sentiment trajectory line tracking the shift from Neutral to Critical across the observation window.



While the network, table and temporal views answer what happened and when, Objective 4 requires the analyst to weigh the evidence as a whole. This panel addresses that directly.
The evidence is organised into four layers: the public timeline, behavioural anomalies, the intent chain reconstructed from internal state text, and anonymous channel authorship. Each layer can be admitted or excluded using toggle controls. A verdict readout updates as layers are admitted, answering two questions side by side: who put the merger into the public domain, and whether any agent engaged in deliberate misconduct.
The design choice is deliberate. Admitting only some layers reproduces the partial conclusions that single perspective analyses reach, while admitting all four shows how the verdict changes when the full evidence is considered. The readout logic is a simple mapping from layer combinations to verdict text, keeping the panel computationally light while making the analytical point that partial evidence produces confident but incomplete answers.







The following R packages are used in this project:
Data Ingestion & Wrangling

jsonlite: Parsing the nested JSON communications data
tidyverse: Core data manipulation and transformation
lubridate: Parsing and handling ISO datetime fields
zoo: Rolling window calculations for behavioural trend analysis

Text & Sentiment Analysis

tidytext: Tokenisation and text feature extraction
sentimentr: Sentence-level sentiment scoring of agent messages

Visualisation

ggplot2: Core static visualisations and annotated timelines
plotly: Interactive chart rendering
patchwork: Composing multi-panel ggplot figures
ggrepel: Non-overlapping labels on scatter and timeline plots
scales: Axis formatting and colour scaling
kableExtra: Styled summary tables

Network Analysis

igraph: Graph construction and network metrics
visNetwork: Interactive network visualisations

Shiny App

shiny: Interactive application framework
shinyWidgets: Extended UI input controls

Reproducibility & Testing

renv: Locking package versions so all collaborators run identical environments
shinytest2: Automated testing of the Shiny application
